data.title: CVR Energy outlines $200M–$240M capital plan for 2026 while expanding WCS processing and M&A focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CVR Energy (CVI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Mark Pytosh, President and CEO, reported consolidated net income of $90 million for the year and EBITDA of $591 million, highlighting, "At the segment level, we generated EBITDA of $411 million in the Petroleum segment, $211 million in the Fertilizer segment and a loss of $22 million in the Renewable segment." He noted that fourth quarter results were impacted by accelerated depreciation from the reversion of the Wynnewood renewable diesel unit and extended downtime at the Coffeyville fertilizer facility.
  • CEO Pytosh emphasized strategic priorities: "Our primary focus will continue to be the safe and reliable operations of our facilities...we are reevaluating our commercial optimization opportunities to drive margin capture improvement in the petroleum segment...we plan to take a more proactive approach in pursuing opportunities to expand our asset footprint."
  • Pytosh introduced new Chief Commercial Officer, Travis Capps, bringing over 30 years of refining and petrochemical leadership, stating, "We're excited to have Travis leading our commercial team as we look to better optimize our refining portfolio."
  • Dane Neumann, Executive VP and CFO, reported, "For the fourth quarter of 2025, our net loss attributable to CVR shareholders was $110 million, losses per share were $1.10, and EBITDA was $51 million." Neumann outlined key impacts, including unfavorable inventory valuation and changes in RFS liability.

Outlook

  • Neumann projected full-year 2026 consolidated capital spending of $200 million to $240 million and petroleum segment turnaround spending of $15 million to $20 million, with growth capital spending of $75 million to $90 million in 2026, "expected to be slightly elevated relative to the past few years as we hit the peak spending year for the alkylation project at Wynnewood along with a host of reliability and debottlenecking projects in the Fertilizer segment."
  • For Q1 2026, expected Petroleum segment throughput is 200,000 to 215,000 barrels per day, direct operating expenses of $110 million to $120 million, and capital spending between $30 million and $35 million. Fertilizer segment ammonia utilization rate is aimed at 95% to 100%, with capital spending between $25 million and $30 million.
  • Management cited constructive market fundamentals for refining and fertilizer, referencing, "Global refining capacity additions are set to slow down in 2026 and 2027...refined product demand growth is expected to remain steady, particularly for diesel."

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 consolidated net loss was $116 million and EBITDA was $51 million. Adjusted EBITDA was $91 million, with adjusted losses per share of $0.80.
  • Petroleum segment adjusted EBITDA was $73 million with combined throughput of approximately 218,000 barrels per day and crude utilization at 97%. Realized margin adjusted for inventory and RFS impacts was $9.92 per barrel, with a 44% capture rate on the Group 3 2-1-1 benchmark.
  • RIN prices averaged $6.05 per barrel, with net RINs expense for the quarter at $90 million. Estimated accrued RFS obligation was $72 million as of December 31.
  • Renewable segment adjusted EBITDA was breakeven for Q4, following the cessation of the renewable diesel unit at Wynnewood.
  • Fertilizer segment adjusted EBITDA was $20 million, with ammonia utilization rate at 64% due to the Coffeyville facility's delayed start-up.
  • Cash flow from operations was breakeven; free cash flow was a use of $55 million. The company ended the quarter with $511 million in cash.

Q&A

  • Manav Gupta, UBS: Asked about a "more persuasive approach to M&A" and asset expansion focus. Neumann explained, "We're looking at both sides of the business, so both our refining business and our fertilizer business...any deal that we would consider has to be accretive to our shareholders or our unitholders."
  • Gupta, UBS: On term loan paydown vs. dividend, Neumann said free cash flow and deleveraging remain priorities, noting, "We don't believe that we have to be back to our base $1 billion target before a dividend can return...we want a dividend that's going to be sustainable in any part of the cycle."
  • Matthew Blair, TPH: Inquired about ramping up WCS runs at Coffeyville. Neumann explained, "When Maduro was removed in Venezuela, that started changing the dynamics in the Western Canadian market...the best economic value of the barrel was to run it at Coffeyville rather than shipping it all down to the Gulf Coast."
  • Blair, TPH: Asked about RIN prices. Neumann said, "RIN obligation at Wynnewood is...2 to 3x what we pay everybody who works at the facility...we are trying to blend more, we're trying to take steps to reduce our overall exposure."
  • [Alexa Petrick], Goldman Sachs: Questioned capture rates and jet fuel production at Coffeyville. Pytosh said, "We're pretty early and not ready to get targets...it's really, from my perspective, a cultural shift where we are constantly looking for those margin capture opportunities."
  • [Alexa Petrick], Goldman Sachs: On product pipelines to the West Coast, Neumann stated, "I'm very optimistic about the Mid-Continent for the next several years...the upside for our company in the Mid-Con is very good."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism about M&A and dividends, with persistent focus on RIN-related costs and margin capture. The tone was probing but constructive, especially regarding growth and capital allocation strategies.
  • Management maintained a disciplined and pragmatic tone, emphasizing operational reliability, financial flexibility, and disciplined capital deployment. Pytosh and Neumann repeatedly stressed sustainable growth and cautious optimism. The language was confident but measured, with an emphasis on maintaining financial strength and flexibility.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone under new leadership was somewhat more proactive on M&A and asset optimization, while analysts remained focused on returns and operational risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for capital spending rose to $200 million to $240 million for 2026 from the prior year's range of $180 million to $200 million.
  • Strategic focus shifted under new CEO Pytosh toward more proactive asset expansion and margin optimization, including increased WCS processing at Coffeyville.
  • Analysts maintained focus on dividend reinstatement, leverage targets, and operational cost controls, echoing concerns from prior quarters.
  • Management confidence in market fundamentals and margin opportunities increased, with greater emphasis on expanding the asset footprint and commercial optimization.
  • Fertilizer segment guidance improved for Q1 2026, with ammonia utilization expected to recover above 95% following Q4 downtime.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited RIN price volatility and regulatory uncertainty as significant cost pressures, particularly for Wynnewood. Neumann highlighted, "RIN obligation at Wynnewood is our financial obligation is 2 to 3x what we pay everybody who works at the facility."
  • Downtime at the Coffeyville fertilizer facility due to third-party start-up issues and the cessation of renewable diesel operations at Wynnewood were identified as recent operational challenges.
  • Management indicated ongoing efforts to blend more or acquire blending capacity to mitigate RIN expense, while maintaining discipline in M&A and capital allocation.

Final Takeaway

CVR Energy’s fourth quarter call showcased a strategic shift under new CEO Mark Pytosh, with a sharpened focus on operational reliability, disciplined capital allocation, and proactive asset expansion. The company outlined an increased 2026 capital spending plan and plans to ramp up WCS processing at Coffeyville. Management addressed persistent RIN-related cost pressures and emphasized a commitment to sustainable dividends, while maintaining confidence in constructive market fundamentals and improved margin opportunities in both the refining and fertilizer segments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Aritzia acquires the Fred Segal brand as it accelerates its U.S. push
data.description:
Aritzia (ATZAF) (ATZ:CA) has acquired the Fred Segal brand, according to Women's Wear Daily. The deal includes the intellectual property and trademarks from Fred Segal Family LLC and  a lease on the original flagship location on Melrose Avenue in Los Angeles. Financial terms were not disclosed. 

The transaction was described by WWD as a purchase of the Fred Segal fashion and lifestyle brand rather than a deal for a broad store base. The seller had owned the core trademarks and licensed them to operators and partners, and Aritzia’s (ATZAF) (ATZ:CA) move transfers those brand rights while pairing them with control of the iconic Melrose flagship, which was damaged in a recent storm and will now be restored and repositioned. 

The broad view on the deal is that it supports Aritzia's (ATZAF) (ATZ:CA) push to accelerate its U.S. expansion and diversify beyond its namesake banner. Aritzia (ATZAF) (ATZ:CA) plans to revive and reimagine Fred Segal for a new generation by combining the brand’s heritage with its own everyday luxury retail capabilities. The Melrose site could be used as an experiential destination and lifestyle hub rather than a conventional apparel store.

data.title: Airline stocks hit as Middle East tensions drive oil to 7-month high
data.description:
Airline stocks are under pressure Thursday as escalating tensions in the Middle East push oil prices to a seven-month high, heightening concerns over fuel costs and profit margins.

As the U.S. deploys another aircraft carrier to the region amid stalled nuclear talks with Iran, the potential for a U.S. strike against Tehran as soon as this weekend pushed the price of a barrel of crude oil up another $2 on Thursday.

For airlines, the 16% increase in the price of oil from the beginning of the year will undoubtedly be reflected in Q1 results. Fuel accounts for approximately 20% to 30% of an airline’s total operating expenses and can reach as much as 40% of total expenses during extended periods of high fuel costs, making it one of the airline sectors’ largest and most volatile expenses.

While carriers can offset much of this volatility through fuel hedging, they risk locking in elevated prices if geopolitical tensions ease and oil stabilizes, potentially leading to significant losses if they misjudge the longer-term trajectory of fuel costs.

Currently, shares of American Airlines (AAL), United Airlines (UAL), and Delta Air Lines (DAL) are all down more than 5%, JetBlue (JBLU) down 9%, and Alaska Air Group (ALK) shares are down 6%. Accordingly, the U.S. Global Jets ETF (JETS) is down 4%.

data.title: Select Water upgraded at Northland on 'decisively bullish' Q4 results, commentary
data.description:
Select Water Solutions (WTTR) -2.4% in Thursday's trading despite receiving an upgrade to Outperform from Market Perform with an $18 price target, raised from $12, at Northland, citing "decisively bullish" Q4 results and commentary.

Northland analyst Bobby Brooks noted the network effect of Select Water Solutions' (WTTR) infrastructure segment is taking hold along with strong gains in the company's chemicals segment.

Brooks said he views 2026 as a catalyst-rich year for the company and thinks the free cash flow generation ability of the business will begin showing in 2027, following years of growth capital spending for infrastructure projects.

"Combine this with our street-high (and achievable) FY27 estimates, and we see a compelling story," the analyst wrote.

data.title: BrainsWay down after investment in Canadian mental care provider
data.description:
BrainsWay (BWAY) ADRs traded lower on Thursday after the Israeli MedTech announced a minority investment worth up to $2.5M in BrainStim Health, an operator of mental health clinics in Canada.

BWAY, which markets non-invasive neurostimulation treatments for psychiatric disorders, said that as part of an equity financing agreement with BrainStim, it will initially invest $1.0M in the British Columbia-based firm.

With additional milestone-based investments reaching up to $1.5B, BrainsWay (BWAY) will receive a preferred, annually compounding security in BrainStim and secure a minority position in the company. The deal allows for a redemption mechanism for investments.

“Our investment in BrainStim is now the fifth minority-stake investment into growth-oriented clinical service platforms announced by the Company,” CEO Hadar Levy said.

“Partnering with an interventional psychiatry pioneer like BrainsWay only serves to strengthen our value proposition and increase our success,” added BrainStim’s Chief Medical Officer, Venu Karapeddy.

data.title: Huntsman downgraded on valuation at J.P. Morgan after strong YTD run-up
data.description:
Huntsman (HUN) -4.2% in Thursday's trading as J.P. Morgan downgraded the chemicals manufacturer to Neutral from Overweight with a $14 price target, saying the current share price now reasonably discounts a meaningful recovery in its EBITDA over a multi-year period.

JPM analyst Jeffrey Zekauskas noted the polyurethanes business, which accounts for more than half of Huntsman's (HUN) EBITDA, has significant exposure to construction end markets, which have been challenged due to higher interest rates, supply additions, and slower global economic growth.

The analyst said he had believed business conditions would reach a trough in the 2023-24 period, but 2025 was challenged by a backdrop of increased economic and tariff uncertainty, and the company is now looking to 2026 for a recovery.

Huntsman's (HUN) value has historically sharply lifted in environments of declining interest rates and accelerating economic activity, and Zekauskas said there should be room for price and volume gains if growth accelerates in the domestic economy.

Huntsman (HUN) is under-earning and can sharply move up in value with some strength from the construction sector or from lower interest rates, the analyst said.

data.title: Community Health Systems outlines $11.6B–$12.0B 2026 revenue guidance as divestitures reshape portfolio
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Community Health Systems (CYH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Kevin Hammons, President, CEO & Director, opened by expressing gratitude for his appointment as permanent CEO and highlighted that "the fourth quarter was in line with our updated expectations, reflecting sequential margin expansion with higher acuity and a slight improvement in payer mix and continued cost controls." Hammons discussed milestone achievements, including a 13% rise in ER visits over two years in Knoxville following investments, a 20% increase in births at Grandview Medical Center driven by a $10 million investment, a 35% increase in inbound transfers in Carlsbad, and a 16% rise in heart surgeries in Longview, Texas. He emphasized ongoing divestitures, capital structure improvements, and reduced leverage from 7.4x at year-end 2024 to 6.6x at year-end 2025. Hammons stated, “with proceeds from transactions completed or to be completed in 2026, we are creating a path for additional debt reduction and deleveraging.”
  • Jason Johnson, CFO & Executive VP, stated, "For the fourth quarter, CHS delivered results generally consistent with the expectations. The company continued to execute well on the controllable aspects of the business and was able to deliver expansion in adjusted EBITDA margin on a sequential basis. thus achieving the midpoint of our updated guidance for the full year 2025."

Outlook

  • Jason Johnson outlined initial 2026 guidance: net revenue of $11.6 billion to $12.0 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $1.34 billion to $1.49 billion, cash flows from operations of $600 million to $700 million, and capital expenditures of $350 million to $400 million. Johnson explained that the lower guidance reflects the impact of completed and pending divestitures as well as one-time or out-of-period items that benefited 2025 results. He noted, “The guidance range with net revenue and adjusted EBITDA both coming in below full year 2025 levels reflects the impact of divestitures completed in 2025 and those that have been announced and have been or are expected to be completed in early 2026.”
  • The company’s outlook does not include impacts from any new or enacted state-directed payment programs pending approval, nor any benefits from the rural health transformation program, and only incorporates divestitures already completed or announced.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 2025 was $395 million with a margin of 12.7%. Same-store net revenue increased 2.1% year-over-year, with net revenue per adjusted admission up 2.4%. Same-store inpatient admissions and adjusted admissions each declined by 0.3%, surgeries declined 1.9%, and ED visits dropped 3.6%. Excluding divested Pennsylvania operations, admissions and adjusted admissions were flat, and surgeries were down 0.4% year-over-year. Labor costs remained well managed, with contract labor spend flat and supply expense declining to 14.4% of net revenue. Medical specialist fees rose to $169 million, up 4.6% year-over-year. Cash flows from operations reached $266 million for Q4 and $543 million for the full year, with adjusted free cash flows at $150 million. Notably, the company received $91 million in contingent consideration and $152 million in net cash proceeds from divestitures, using part of these funds to redeem $223 million of senior secured notes. Net debt at year-end was $10.1 billion, with a reduction to $9.2 billion expected post-Huntsville divestiture.

Q&A

  • Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies: Asked about further divestitures and portfolio strategy. Kevin Hammons responded they are “getting…closer to the end of our programmatic divestitures,” with only a couple of potential transactions in early-stage discussion and expressed comfort with the current portfolio.
  • Albert Rice, UBS: Inquired about portfolio performance differences and capital spending. Hammons explained that smaller hospitals are integrated into networks and noted, “we're spending more per hospital going forward,” aided by improved cash flow, and highlighted investments in AI for cost savings and clinical improvements.
  • Ben Hendrix, RBC: Asked for a revenue bridge post-divestitures. Jason Johnson detailed removals of $210 million to $230 million net revenue from 2025 sales and about $1 billion from early 2026 divestitures, noting, “the jump-off point for 2025 net revenue was about $11.2 billion.”
  • Jason Cassorla, Guggenheim: Queried on EBITDA growth drivers after exchange headwinds. Johnson attributed growth to a 2.5%–3.5% rate increase and the rest from mix, acuity, and volume, with Hammons adding that a 4% Medicare rate increase would be beneficial.
  • Joshua Raskin, Nephron: Sought details on ERP and revenue optimization. Hammons reported $50 million in savings from the ERP rollout and anticipated ongoing efficiency gains from AI integration and system maturation.
  • Andrew Mok, Barclays: Probed on ACA-related EBITDA headwinds. Johnson clarified the $20 million to $30 million EBITDA impact is offset by low margin on exchange business and cost avoidance.
  • Stephen Baxter, Wells Fargo: Questioned decremental margins from exchange attrition and volume growth assumptions. Johnson projected low single-digit same-store volume growth for 2026, with Hammons expecting Medicare and commercial business to drive improvements.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised recurring concerns on the sustainability and impact of divestitures, portfolio composition, and ACA-driven volume/margin headwinds, showing a neutral to slightly cautious tone with probing on margin sustainability and capital allocation.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing strategic progress and financial discipline. In Q&A, responses were measured and occasionally hedged with phrases like “we believe” and references to uncertainty regarding ACA impacts. Hammons expressed comfort with portfolio quality and openness to opportunistic transactions.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts maintained a pragmatic approach, while management’s tone shifted from transitional optimism to firmer confidence in portfolio strategy and operational execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is lower than 2025 actuals due to the impact of divestitures and absence of one-time benefits recognized in 2025. The focus on deleveraging and operational cost controls continues, with more explicit communication on the end-stage of divestiture strategy. There is greater emphasis on capital investments in core markets and AI-driven efficiencies. Analysts’ questions this quarter centered more on structural changes, margin impacts from ACA enrollment shifts, and capital deployment post-divestitures, contrasting with earlier focus on volume recovery and cash flow inflection. Management’s confidence has solidified, moving from transitional to established leadership.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted persistent upward pressure on medical specialist fees, projecting 5% to 8% growth in 2026, and acknowledged ongoing regulatory and economic disruptions affecting patient behavior and reimbursement uncertainty. ACA enrollment reductions and shifting payer mix are expected to pose headwinds, with estimated $20 million to $30 million EBITDA impact. Analysts flagged potential margin compression from exchange attrition and sought clarity on decremental cost management and capital allocation discipline post-divestitures. Management stressed ongoing cost controls and system investments to mitigate these risks.

Final Takeaway

Community Health Systems enters 2026 with a leaner, more focused hospital portfolio following a series of divestitures, supported by a strengthened capital structure and reduced leverage. Management projects 2026 net revenue between $11.6 billion and $12.0 billion and expects operational improvements, cost controls, and targeted capital investments to offset margin pressures from regulatory changes and payer mix shifts. The leadership expresses confidence in the company’s ability to deliver growth through disciplined execution and continued investment in high-performing core markets, advanced technology, and operational efficiency initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Host Hotels targets $1.77B adjusted EBITDAre in 2026 while signaling capital returns after $1.1B asset sale
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) Q4 2025

Management View

  • James Risoleo, President, CEO & Director, opened the call stating, "2025 was another strong year for Host. We delivered operational improvements across our portfolio driven by rate growth and out-of-room spending, and we continue to successfully allocate capital through dispositions, portfolio reinvestment, share repurchases and dividends." He emphasized that adjusted EBITDAre reached $1,757 million for the year, a 4.6% increase, and highlighted that portfolio performance exceeded initial 2025 guidance by notable margins.
  • Risoleo elaborated on the sale of the Four Seasons Resort Orlando at Walt Disney World Resort and the Four Seasons Resort and Residences Jackson Hole for $1.1 billion, calling it "an opportunistic transaction to create immediate and tangible value for our shareholders." He detailed that the sale price represents an 11% unlevered IRR and an EBITDA multiple "more than 4 turns higher than our company's recent trading multiple."
  • The CEO noted that Host recognized $17 million of net adjusted EBITDAre from the sale of 16 condo units and expects to recognize an additional $20 million to $25 million from remaining units.
  • Risoleo said, "We returned nearly $860 million of capital to shareholders in 2025, including share repurchases." He also highlighted $644 million invested in capital expenditures, with major renovation programs on track.
  • Sourav Ghosh, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Comparable hotel food and beverage revenue for the quarter grew approximately 6%, driven by outlet revenue and banquet contribution per group room night." He added that resort outlet growth was led by Maui and that spa revenue was up 6% while golf revenue grew 14%.

Outlook

  • Management guided for 2026 adjusted EBITDAre midpoint of $1,770 million and expects comparable hotel total RevPAR growth of 2.5% to 4%, with comparable hotel RevPAR growth of 2% to 3.5%. The comparable hotel EBITDA margin is anticipated at 29.2% at the midpoint.
  • Host expects Maui to contribute approximately $120 million of EBITDA in 2026. Capital expenditure guidance is set between $525 million and $625 million, with $250 million to $300 million focused on redevelopment, repositioning, and ROI projects.
  • The company expects to recognize a taxable gain of approximately $500 million from the sale of the two Four Seasons hotels, with intentions to return the gain to shareholders via a special dividend if no accretive acquisition is identified within 45 days.

Financial Results

  • For Q4 2025, Host delivered adjusted EBITDAre of $428 million and adjusted FFO per share of $0.51. Comparable hotel total RevPAR improved 5.4% year-over-year, and comparable hotel RevPAR was up 4.6%.
  • Comparable hotel EBITDA margin for the quarter declined by 30 basis points to 28% due to onetime benefits in the prior year. Ancillary spending remained strong, with food and beverage revenues and out-of-room spending driving performance.
  • The company reported that Maui contributed $111 million of EBITDA for the year, slightly ahead of forecasts. Portfolio outperformed upper tier industry RevPAR growth by approximately 200 basis points for the year.

Q&A

  • Michael Bellisario, Robert W. Baird & Co.: Asked about the buyer pool for high-value assets and future dispositions. Risoleo responded, "The buyer pool for these types of assets is, I think, a lot deeper than people realize...everything is for sale at the right price, and we mean it."
  • David Katz, Jefferies: Requested more detail on the Transformational Capital Program. Risoleo said, "By investing in these assets in a transformational way...we're going to meaningfully increase our yield index and realize mid-teens cash-on-cash returns as a result of our incremental investment."
  • Daniel Politzer, JPMorgan: Inquired about Maui's EBITDA trajectory. Ghosh said, "At this point in time, we feel comfortable with the $120 million. Does that change over the course of the year as we see potential group pace pick up and short-term pick up? Absolutely."
  • Bennett Rose, Citigroup: Questioned if declining CapEx alters dividend strategy. Risoleo replied, "Our objective is to pay out our taxable income and to pay a sustainable dividend going forward."
  • Aryeh Klein, BMO Capital Markets: Asked about acquisitions following asset sales. Risoleo indicated the acquisition market "is better than it was last year, but it's still not robust," and suggested a special dividend is more likely than acquisitions.
  • Cooper Clark, Wells Fargo: Sought clarity on allocation of remaining sale proceeds. Risoleo said, "It's something that we have to take a measured approach to as we always do."
  • Chris Darling, Green Street: Asked about expense outlook. Ghosh responded, "Our expense growth is -- total expense growth is assumed at 3.3% with total revenue growth of 3.3%."
  • Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI: Asked about market tailwinds. Risoleo highlighted benefits from World Cup matches and strong pacing in key markets. Ghosh added, "Hyatt Regency where the group matters meaningfully, we are pacing really strong."
  • Robin Farley, UBS: Pressed on asset types for future acquisitions. Risoleo said the focus is on assets with "meaningful upside opportunities from our asset management platform and our enterprise analytics platform."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on capital allocation, asset sales, and reinvestment outcomes, with questions reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought clarity on strategic direction and confidence in continued performance.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing disciplined capital allocation and value creation. Risoleo used terms like "we are very proud" and "we believe that our diversified portfolio...uniquely position Host to capitalize on future opportunities."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased confidence, with analysts probing more about returns to shareholders and management demonstrating assertiveness in highlighting outperformance and strategic execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance increased for adjusted EBITDAre and RevPAR growth over the previous quarter, with management now projecting $1,770 million adjusted EBITDAre for 2026 compared to prior guidance of $1,730 billion for 2025.
  • The sale of two Four Seasons hotels for $1.1 billion was a significant shift in capital allocation, with proceeds earmarked for shareholder returns or accretive acquisitions—an evolution from a prior focus on reinvestment and selective acquisitions.
  • Analysts shifted from questions about asset trading and renovation returns in Q3 to a greater focus on capital deployment strategies and potential special dividends in Q4.
  • Management tone shifted from cautious optimism to overt confidence, citing "meaningful shareholder value" and portfolio outperformance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged the potential challenge of not identifying an accretive acquisition for the $500 million taxable gain, which could result in a special dividend rather than portfolio reinvestment.
  • Expense growth, particularly wage increases (expected at 5% in 2026), continues to be a focus, though management cited productivity initiatives and insurance cost declines as partial offsets.
  • Analysts raised concerns about portfolio deployment, future disposition appetite, and sustainability of RevPAR and margin growth.

Final Takeaway

Host Hotels & Resorts closed 2025 with strong operational and financial results, highlighted by a major asset sale and plans to return capital to shareholders if no attractive acquisitions arise. Management projects continued RevPAR and EBITDAre growth in 2026, supported by a robust balance sheet, portfolio reinvestment, and productivity initiatives, while remaining flexible in capital allocation to maximize total shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hotel chains inking deals with AI firms have investors skittish on Expedia, Booking Holdings
data.description:
Booking Holdings (BKNG) and Expedia (EXPE) fell sharply on Thursday as investors digested news that Wyndham is striking partnerships with Google, Anthropic, and ChatGPT. BTIG analyst Jake Fuller said that development has investors worried that online travel sites sit on the wrong side of the AI risk spectrum. 

"The issue from our vantage is that multiple chains have pointed to agentic bookings w/the major LLMs & investors have always viewed the chains as the industry. The fear is that AI potentially disintermediates OTAs by going direct, and the headlines are feeding into that. Our Take: Chains represent a small piece of the global accommodations; BKNG has limited chain exposure & we continue to see OTAs as partners of choice given the breadth of supply, global payment networks, service infrastructure & hefty advertising budgets," warned Fuller.

Notably, Marriott International (MAR) has also disclosed talks about developing agentic booking capabilities with Google (GOOG).

Booking Holdings (BKNG) slid 7.7% with the company's Q4 earnings report also in the mix. Expedia (EXPE) was down 2.2%.

data.title: Broadstone targets $350M–$500M rolling build-to-suit pipeline as portfolio remains 99% leased
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Broadstone Net Lease, Inc. (BNL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO John Moragne described 2025 as "pivotal in terms of proving out the promise of this company and our strategy" and highlighted that Broadstone delivered $1.49 of AFFO per share, representing 4.2% year-over-year growth. He emphasized "solid in-place portfolio performance" with a 99% leased rate and 99.8% of rents collected, and outlined deployment of $748.4 million in investments, including $429.9 million in new property acquisitions and $209.3 million in build-to-suit developments. Moragne also discussed the company's proactive management of tenant issues, stating, "Despite the headlines, the actual financial impact from tenant situations last year was limited with bad debt for 2025, amounting to only 31 basis points." He addressed the American Signature bankruptcy, noting, "We realized no bad debt throughout the process, and we now have a strong retail furniture operator in all 6 of our locations." On Red Lobster, he explained that Broadstone is evaluating potential sale or lease paths to reduce exposure, while reiterating 2026 AFFO guidance of $1.53 to $1.57 per share, "or 4% at the midpoint."
  • President & COO Ryan Albano reported that 2025 saw approximately $4.5 million of ABR commenced from build-to-suit projects, with current in-process developments totaling $345 million and offering estimated initial cash yields of 7.4%. He also highlighted a pipeline of $142 million in advanced-stage projects under executed LOIs and noted the company's ongoing focus on Project Triboro, an industrial development with "potential transaction structures ranging from powered land to powered shell configurations."
  • CFO Kevin Fennell stated, "During the quarter, we generated adjusted funds from operations of $75.8 million or $0.38 per share, a 5.6% increase over Q4 of 2024." He emphasized cost management, noting, "Core G&A was well managed once again during the year, with expenses totaling $7 million during the fourth quarter and $28.7 million for the full year, down 2% year-over-year." Fennell discussed financial flexibility, with pro forma leverage of 5.8x and over $700 million available on the revolver, as well as a recent amendment to term loans resulting in lower rates and extended maturities. He explained, "We are reiterating our 2026 per share guidance range of $1.53 to $1.57 per share with the following key assumptions: investment volume between $500 million and $625 million, disposition volume between $75 million and $100 million, and finally, core G&A between $30 million and $31 million."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated 2026 AFFO guidance of $1.53 to $1.57 per share, with key assumptions including investment volume between $500 million and $625 million, disposition volume between $75 million and $100 million, and core G&A between $30 million and $31 million. The company expects $350 million of build-to-suit developments to reach stabilization in 2026, adding nearly $26 million of incremental ABR. The pipeline is maintained at $350 million to $500 million for build-to-suit projects.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted funds from operations for Q4 2025 were $75.8 million or $0.38 per share, representing a 5.6% increase over Q4 2024. For the full year, AFFO was $296.3 million or $1.49 per share, a 4.2% increase year-over-year. Portfolio ended the year 99% leased with 99.8% of rents collected. Lost rent totaled 31 basis points for the year, down from 67 basis points in 2024. Core G&A for the year was $28.7 million, down 2% year-over-year. The company raised approximately $43 million of equity on a forward basis since November.

Q&A

  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan, asked about competition in build-to-suit. John Moragne responded, "We have not seen an increase in the level of competition on the deals that we're looking at... So certainly more attention and activity in the area, but it's not impacting the top of our funnel or the way that we're able to source deals."
  • Eric Borden, BMO, inquired about recycling assets and UNFI proceeds. Moragne explained the focus is on timing of deployment rather than the mix, and regarding leverage, "we're still dancing around those levels and evaluating what that next capital source is." On internal growth, Moragne said, "relying on that 2% as a go-forward assumption is reasonable, and then you'll see us move around that and really move that higher over time."
  • Upal Rana, KeyBanc, asked about Red Lobster exposure. Moragne stated, "It's early days in this discussion... We're having good productive conversations... We're open to ideas for re-leasing, moving on from some of the sites, selling them... it has to be something that's mutually beneficial."
  • Caitlin Burrows, Goldman Sachs, asked about build-to-suit pipeline and Claire's lease. Moragne clarified the $350 million to $500 million target is rolling and mostly repeat business. Regarding Claire's, "no termination fee because of the bankruptcy history... any impact from that has already been baked into our view from a guidance standpoint."
  • Ronald Kamdem, Morgan Stanley, asked about Project Triboro leasing. Ryan Albano shared, "we'll officially be in market, looking for tenant and leasing activity in the fairly near term, I call it, first half of this year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on clarity about strategy, asset recycling, and tenant exposure, seeking details on competition and future growth.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in prepared remarks, with direct responses and openness about risks, using phrases such as "we remain highly confident in our ability to navigate our exposure" and "we are reiterating our 2026 AFFO guidance."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management continues to display confidence, while analysts persist in probing around tenant risks and capital allocation, but with less urgency than before.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management maintained guidance, with a continued focus on the build-to-suit strategy and proactive tenant management. The AFFO guidance range remained, but the emphasis shifted to embedding long-term growth through the established pipeline. Analysts' focus shifted from clarifying the implications of tenant events (Claire's, At Home) in Q3 to seeking details about capital recycling, Project Triboro, and the competitive landscape for build-to-suit. Key metrics such as lease rates and bad debt improved, while the tone on capital markets activity became more optimistic due to improved market sentiment and a slightly higher equity multiple.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing monitoring of Red Lobster, which "currently represents approximately 1.3% of total ABR across 18 sites," with strategies to gradually reduce exposure. Tenant events like the American Signature bankruptcy were managed with no bad debt incurred. The company is cautious about market risks impacting tenant credit and remains disciplined on acquisitions. Project Triboro's success depends on phase execution and power delivery, with alternative paths considered.

Final Takeaway

Broadstone Net Lease emphasized the stability and resilience of its portfolio, with 99% occupancy and minimal bad debt, while maintaining a disciplined investment approach focused on build-to-suit developments and active tenant management. The company reiterated its 2026 AFFO guidance and highlighted the embedded growth in its pipeline, citing Project Triboro and robust build-to-suit opportunities as key drivers for future value creation. Management remains confident in navigating tenant risks and capital allocation as it seeks to enhance shareholder value.
